title: The Memory of Sidewalk Trees
text_zh: 这条路上的法国梧桐是一九五六年种的，如今每一棵的树干都粗得需要两个人才能合抱。城市绿化部门的档案里记载着它们的编号、品种和种植日期，但没有记载的是它们各自经历过的那些微小的人间故事。三号树根部的水泥地面有一道被反复修补过的裂缝，那是九十年代一辆失控的自行车撞上来留下的痕迹——骑车的是个赶着去考试的大学生，据说他爬起来之后第一件事不是检查自己有没有受伤，而是确认书包里的准考证还在不在。十七号树的树干上有一处凹陷，那是二零零三年非典期间，社区工作人员在树上钉宣传栏留下的钉孔，十多年过去，树皮已经几乎把它包裹住了，只剩下一个浅浅的酒窝。最让我感触的是四十二号树下的一条长椅。这条长椅是一对老夫妻捐赠的，椅背上嵌着一块小铜牌，刻着「献给在这棵树下相识的五十年」。我后来打听到，老先生已经去世了，老太太每天傍晚还是会来这条长椅上坐一会儿，有时候带一本书，有时候什么都不带，就看着来来往往的行人发呆。管理这条路的园林工人跟我说，他每次修剪四十二号树的时候都会格外小心，尽量不让树枝的阴影偏离那条长椅。「我不知道她在等什么，」他说，「但我能做的就是让那片树荫一直在。」
text_pinyin: Zhè tiáo lù shàng de Fǎguó wútóng shì yī jiǔ wǔ liù nián zhòng de, rújīn měi yī kē de shùgàn dōu cū de xūyào liǎng gè rén cái néng hébào. Chéngshì lǜhuà bùmén de dàng'àn lǐ jìzǎi zhe tāmen de biānhào, pǐnzhǒng hé zhòngzhí rìqī, dàn méiyǒu jìzǎi de shì tāmen gèzì jīnglì guò de nàxiē wēixiǎo de rénjiān gùshi. Sān hào shù gēnbù de shuǐní dìmiàn yǒu yī dào bèi fǎnfù xiūbǔ guò de lièfèng, nà shì jiǔshí niándài yī liàng shīkòng de zìxíngchē zhuàng shànglái liú xià de hénjì——qí chē de shì gè gǎn zhe qù kǎoshì de dàxuéshēng, jùshuō tā pá qǐlái zhīhòu dì yī jiàn shì bú shì jiǎnchá zìjǐ yǒu méiyǒu shòushāng, ér shì quèrèn shūbāo lǐ de zhǔnkǎozhèng hái zài bú zài. Shíqī hào shù de shùgàn shàng yǒu yī chù āoxiàn, nà shì èr líng líng sān nián fēidiǎn qījiān, shèqū gōngzuò rényuán zài shù shàng dīng xuānchuánlán liú xià de dīngkǒng, shí duō nián guòqù, shùpí yǐjīng jīhū bǎ tā bāoguǒ zhù le, zhǐ shèng xià yī gè qiǎnqiǎn de jiǔwō. Zuì ràng wǒ gǎnchù de shì sìshí'èr hào shù xià de yī tiáo chángyǐ. Zhè tiáo chángyǐ shì yī duì lǎo fūqī juānzèng de, yǐbèi shàng qiàn zhe yī kuài xiǎo tóngpái, kè zhe「xiàn gěi zài zhè kē shù xià xiāngshí de wǔshí nián」. Wǒ hòulái dǎting dào, lǎo xiānshēng yǐjīng qùshì le, lǎotàitai měi tiān bàngwǎn háishi huì lái zhè tiáo chángyǐ shàng zuò yīhuìr, yǒu shíhou dài yī běn shū, yǒu shíhou shénme dōu bú dài, jiù kàn zhe láilái wǎngwǎng de xíngrén fādāi. Guǎnlǐ zhè tiáo lù de yuánlín gōngrén gēn wǒ shuō, tā měi cì xiūjiǎn sìshí'èr hào shù de shíhou dōu huì géwài xiǎoxīn, jǐnliàng bú ràng shùzhī de yīnyǐng piānlí nà tiáo chángyǐ. 「Wǒ bù zhīdào tā zài děng shénme,」tā shuō, 「dàn wǒ néng zuò de jiù shì ràng nà piàn shùyīn yīzhí zài.」
text_en: The plane trees along this road were planted in 1956; today every trunk is so thick it takes two people to wrap their arms around it. The city's greening department archives record their numbers, species, and planting dates, but what they don't record are the small human stories each tree has witnessed. On the concrete at the base of tree number three there's a crack that has been repaired repeatedly—the mark left in the nineties by an out-of-control bicycle that crashed into it. The rider was a college student rushing to an exam; reportedly, the first thing he did after getting up wasn't checking whether he was injured, but confirming whether his exam admission ticket was still in his backpack. On the trunk of tree seventeen there's a depression—a nail hole left during the 2003 SARS outbreak when community workers nailed a bulletin board to the tree. More than a decade later, the bark has almost swallowed it, leaving only a shallow dimple. What touches me most is the bench beneath tree forty-two. This bench was donated by an elderly couple, with a small brass plaque set into the backrest reading: 'Dedicated to the fifty years since we met under this tree.' I later learned that the old gentleman had passed away. The old woman still comes to sit on this bench every evening—sometimes with a book, sometimes with nothing at all, just watching the passersby in a daze. The landscaping worker who manages this stretch told me he is always extra careful when pruning tree forty-two, trying his best to keep the shadow of its branches from drifting away from that bench. 'I don't know what she's waiting for,' he said, 'but what I can do is make sure that patch of shade is always there.'

title: The Etiquette of Elevator Silence
text_zh: 搬进这栋三十二层的高楼之后，我开始研究一种特殊的社会现象——电梯里的沉默。这种沉默不同于任何其他场合的安静，它有着自己的规则、层次和微妙的变体。最常见的是「礼貌性沉默」，发生在两个互不相识的邻居之间：进门时点头微笑，然后各自盯着楼层数字跳动，全程不发一言。这种沉默是城市人际关系中最精巧的发明之一——它既表达了「我看到你了」的基本礼貌，又维护了「我不会打扰你」的安全距离。然后是「尴尬性沉默」，通常出现在刚吵过架的邻居之间。空气中弥漫着一种可以触摸的紧张感，两个人恨不得把自己缩进电梯的对角线两端。最耐人寻味的是「默契性沉默」，存在于那些住了很多年、彼此熟悉却从未深交的老邻居之间。他们不说话，不是因为无话可说，而是因为已经不需要说——一个眼神、一个表情的细微变化，就足以传达「今天累了」或者「最近还好吗」这样的信息。有一次停电，电梯困住了我和六楼的陈叔。黑暗中我们被迫打破了多年的沉默惯例，第一次真正聊了起来。他说他其实很早就想跟我说，他挺喜欢我阳台上种的薄荷，每次电梯门开的时候都能闻到一点点清香。我说我也一直想告诉他，他每天早上在楼道里练太极拳的声音非常好听。电来了，电梯门开了，我们相视一笑，然后继续回到各自的沉默里。但那个沉默已经不一样了——它变成了一种被温暖填充过的安静。
text_pinyin: Bān jìn zhè dòng sānshí'èr céng de gāolóu zhīhòu, wǒ kāishǐ yánjiū yī zhǒng tèshū de shèhuì xiànxiàng——diàntī lǐ de chénmò. Zhè zhǒng chénmò bùtóng yú rènhé qítā chǎnghé de ānjìng, tā yǒu zhe zìjǐ de guīzé, céngcì hé wēimiào de biàntǐ. Zuì chángjiàn de shì「lǐmàoxìng chénmò」, fāshēng zài liǎng gè hù bù xiāngshí de línjū zhījiān: jìn mén shí diǎntóu wēixiào, ránhòu gèzì dīng zhe lóucéng shùzì tiàodòng, quánchéng bù fā yī yán. Zhè zhǒng chénmò shì chéngshì rénjì guānxi zhōng zuì jīngqiǎo de fāmíng zhī yī——tā jì biǎodá le「wǒ kàn dào nǐ le」de jīběn lǐmào, yòu wéihù le「wǒ bú huì dǎrǎo nǐ」de ānquán jùlí. Ránhòu shì「gāngàxìng chénmò」, tōngcháng chūxiàn zài gāng chǎo guò jià de línjū zhījiān. Kōngqì zhōng mímàn zhe yī zhǒng kěyǐ chùmō de jǐnzhānggǎn, liǎng gè rén hènbude bǎ zìjǐ suō jìn diàntī de duìjiǎoxiàn liǎng duān. Zuì nàirénxúnwèi de shì「mòqìxìng chénmò」, cúnzài yú nàxiē zhù le hěn duō nián, bǐcǐ shúxī què cóng wèi shēnjiāo de lǎo línjū zhījiān. Tāmen bù shuōhuà, bú shì yīnwèi wú huà kě shuō, ér shì yīnwèi yǐjīng bù xūyào shuō——yī gè yǎnshén, yī gè biǎoqíng de xìwēi biànhuà, jiù zúyǐ chuándá「jīntiān lèi le」huòzhě「zuìjìn hái hǎo ma」zhèyàng de xìnxī. Yǒu yī cì tíngdiàn, diàntī kùn zhù le wǒ hé liù lóu de Chén shū. Hēi'àn zhōng wǒmen bèi pò dǎpò le duō nián de chénmò guànlì, dì yī cì zhēnzhèng liáo le qǐlái. Tā shuō tā qíshí hěn zǎo jiù xiǎng gēn wǒ shuō, tā tǐng xǐhuān wǒ yángtái shàng zhòng de bòhe, měi cì diàntī mén kāi de shíhou dōu néng wén dào yīdiǎndiǎn qīngxiāng. Wǒ shuō wǒ yě yīzhí xiǎng gàosu tā, tā měi tiān zǎoshang zài lóudào lǐ liàn tàijíquán de shēngyīn fēicháng hǎotīng. Diàn lái le, diàntī mén kāi le, wǒmen xiāngshì yī xiào, ránhòu jìxù huí dào gèzì de chénmò lǐ. Dàn nàge chénmò yǐjīng bù yīyàng le——tā biànchéng le yī zhǒng bèi wēnnuǎn tiánchōng guò de ānjìng.
text_en: After moving into this thirty-two-story tower, I began studying a peculiar social phenomenon—silence in the elevator. This silence differs from quiet in any other setting; it has its own rules, layers, and subtle variations. The most common is 'polite silence,' occurring between two neighbors who don't know each other: a nod and smile upon entering, then each staring at the floor numbers as they tick by, no words exchanged the entire ride. This silence is one of the most ingenious inventions in urban interpersonal relations—it expresses the basic courtesy of 'I see you' while maintaining the safe distance of 'I won't bother you.' Then there is 'awkward silence,' typically appearing between neighbors who have just quarreled. A palpable tension fills the air, and both parties wish they could shrink into opposite diagonal corners of the elevator. Most thought-provoking is 'tacit silence,' existing between old neighbors who have lived here for many years, familiar with each other yet never close. They don't speak not because there's nothing to say, but because they no longer need to—a glance, a subtle shift in expression is enough to convey 'tired today' or 'doing all right lately?' Once, during a power outage, the elevator trapped me with Uncle Chen from the sixth floor. In the darkness we were forced to break years of silence protocol and truly talked for the first time. He said he'd actually wanted to tell me for a long time that he quite likes the mint I grow on my balcony—every time the elevator doors open he catches a faint whiff of its fragrance. I said I'd also been meaning to tell him that the sounds of his tai chi practice in the hallway each morning are really lovely to hear. The power came back, the elevator doors opened, we exchanged a smile, and then returned to our respective silences. But that silence was no longer the same—it had become a quietness filled with warmth.

title: The Autobiography of a Bench
text_zh: 公园里有一条被坐得发亮的木质长椅，管理员说它是一九九八年安装的，至今更换过两次椅面木条、三次扶手、一次椅腿螺栓。如果把它比作一个人，那它大约相当于做过两次骨科手术、换过三副假牙、还打了一次石膏。但它的「灵魂」——那个焊接在地面上的铸铁底座——从未被替换过。我常常想象，如果这条长椅会说话，它会讲述怎样的故事。清晨五点，第一个坐上来的总是练完功的老人，他们带着保温杯和收音机，在椅面上留下一层薄薄的茶渍。上午九点左右，年轻的妈妈带着孩子来晒太阳，婴儿车的轮子总会碾过长椅前面同一片泥地，久而久之形成了两道浅浅的车辙。午后最安静的时候，偶尔有人在这里午睡，头枕着扶手，脸上盖着报纸，鞋子脱了放在椅子底下——那是对一个公共空间能给予的最大信任。傍晚是长椅最忙碌的时段，情侣、下棋的老人、刷手机的年轻人轮番使用同一个座位，就像城市生活的流水席。最让我触动的是那些独自来坐的人。他们不看手机，不看书，不和任何人说话，就那样安静地坐着，眼睛望着远处或者什么都不望。我觉得长椅最重要的功能不是供人坐下来做什么，而是允许人坐下来什么都不做——在一个以效率衡量一切的社会里，这种无所事事的权利本身就是一种奢侈。
text_pinyin: Gōngyuán lǐ yǒu yī tiáo bèi zuò de fā liàng de mùzhì chángyǐ, guǎnlǐyuán shuō tā shì yī jiǔ jiǔ bā nián ānzhuāng de, zhìjīn gēnghuàn guò liǎng cì yǐmiàn mùtiáo, sān cì fúshǒu, yī cì yǐtuǐ luóshuān. Rúguǒ bǎ tā bǐzuò yī gè rén, nà tā dàyuē xiāngdāng yú zuò guò liǎng cì gǔkē shǒushù, huàn guò sān fù jiǎyá, hái dǎ le yī cì shígāo. Dàn tā de「línghún」——nàge hànjiē zài dìmiàn shàng de zhùtiě dǐzuò——cóng wèi bèi tìhuàn guò. Wǒ chángcháng xiǎngxiàng, rúguǒ zhè tiáo chángyǐ huì shuōhuà, tā huì jiǎngshù zěnyàng de gùshi. Qīngchén wǔ diǎn, dì yī gè zuò shànglái de zǒng shì liàn wán gōng de lǎorén, tāmen dài zhe bǎowēnbēi hé shōuyīnjī, zài yǐmiàn shàng liú xià yī céng báo báo de chá zì. Shàngwǔ jiǔ diǎn zuǒyòu, niánqīng de māma dài zhe háizi lái shài tàiyáng, yīng'ér chē de lúnzi zǒng huì niǎn guò chángyǐ qiánmiàn tóngyī piàn ní dì, jiǔ ér jiǔ zhī xíngchéng le liǎng dào qiǎnqiǎn de chēzhé. Wǔhòu zuì ānjìng de shíhou, ǒu'ěr yǒu rén zài zhèlǐ wǔshuì, tóu zhěn zhe fúshǒu, liǎn shàng gài zhe bàozhǐ, xiézi tuō le fàng zài yǐzi dǐxia——nà shì duì yī gè gōnggòng kōngjiān néng jǐyǔ de zuìdà xìnrèn. Bàngwǎn shì chángyǐ zuì mánglù de shíduàn, qínglǚ, xià qí de lǎorén, shuā shǒujī de niánqīngrén lúnfān shǐyòng tóngyī gè zuòwèi, jiù xiàng chéngshì shēnghuó de liúshuǐxí. Zuì ràng wǒ chùdòng de shì nàxiē dúzì lái zuò de rén. Tāmen bú kàn shǒujī, bú kàn shū, bù hé rènhé rén shuōhuà, jiù nàyàng ānjìng de zuò zhe, yǎnjīng wàng zhe yuǎnchù huòzhě shénme dōu bú wàng. Wǒ juéde chángyǐ zuì zhòngyào de gōngnéng bú shì gōng rén zuò xiàlái zuò shénme, ér shì yǔnxǔ rén zuò xiàlái shénme dōu bú zuò——zài yī gè yǐ xiàolǜ héngliáng yīqiè de shèhuì lǐ, zhè zhǒng wú suǒ shì shì de quánlì běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng shēchǐ.
text_en: In the park there is a wooden bench polished to a sheen from years of sitting. The groundskeeper says it was installed in 1998 and has since had its seat slats replaced twice, its armrests three times, and its leg bolts once. If you compared it to a person, that would be roughly the equivalent of two orthopedic surgeries, three sets of dentures, and one plaster cast. But its 'soul'—the cast-iron base welded into the ground—has never been replaced. I often imagine what stories this bench would tell if it could speak. At five in the morning, the first to sit down are always the elders who've finished their morning exercises, carrying thermoses and radios, leaving a thin film of tea stains on the seat. Around nine, young mothers bring their children to bask in the sun, the stroller wheels always rolling over the same patch of dirt in front of the bench, forming over time two shallow ruts. During the quietest part of the afternoon, someone occasionally naps here, head pillowed on an armrest, newspaper draped over the face, shoes slipped off and placed under the seat—the greatest trust one can give a public space. Evening is the bench's busiest period: couples, chess-playing elders, phone-scrolling young people take turns using the same seat, like a banquet buffet of city life. What moves me most are those who come to sit alone. They don't look at their phones, don't read, don't talk to anyone—they just sit quietly, eyes gazing into the distance or at nothing at all. I believe the bench's most important function is not providing a place for people to sit down and do something, but permitting people to sit down and do nothing—in a society that measures everything by efficiency, the right to idleness is itself a luxury.

title: The Taxonomy of Window Light
text_zh: 深夜从对面的高楼望过去，每一扇亮着灯的窗户都是一个微型剧场。我曾经花了整整一个冬天，在自己的阳台上观察对面那栋楼的窗灯变化，像一个不带望远镜的业余天文学家，用肉眼辨认着属于人间的星座。暖黄色的灯光最为常见，那通常是客厅的吸顶灯或台灯，意味着有人还在活动——看电视、做家务、或者只是等一个晚归的家人。冷白色的荧光灯光几乎可以确定是在加班——那种灯光有一种不带感情的均匀明亮，像是把黑夜强行切割出一块白天的碎片。最温柔的是那种若有若无的蓝色微光，通常来自手机或平板电脑的屏幕，说明主人已经上床但还没有入睡，正在黑暗中刷着最后几条消息。偶尔会出现一种快速闪烁的彩色光——那是有人在深夜看电影或打游戏。最让我着迷的是那些在凌晨两三点突然亮起的灯。它们总是很突兀，像夜空中划过的流星，让人忍不住猜测背后的原因——是被噩梦惊醒？是婴儿的哭声？还是忽然想起了一件放不下的事？这些灯通常在十几分钟后熄灭，仿佛那个短暂的清醒只是梦境的延伸。我后来意识到，我在做的事情本质上是一种城市版的观星——每一扇窗户都是一颗恒星，每一盏灯都是它此刻的光谱。你无法知道那颗星球上发生了什么具体的故事，但光的颜色、亮度和持续时间已经足以勾勒出一个生活的轮廓。
text_pinyin: Shēnyè cóng duìmiàn de gāolóu wàng guòqù, měi yī shàn liàng zhe dēng de chuānghù dōu shì yī gè wēixíng jùchǎng. Wǒ céngjīng huā le zhěngzhěng yī gè dōngtiān, zài zìjǐ de yángtái shàng guānchá duìmiàn nà dòng lóu de chuāng dēng biànhuà, xiàng yī gè bú dài wàngyuǎnjìng de yèyú tiānwénxuéjiā, yòng ròuyǎn biànrèn zhe shǔyú rénjiān de xīngzuò. Nuǎn huángsè de dēngguāng zuìwéi chángjiàn, nà tōngcháng shì kètīng de xīdǐng dēng huò táidēng, yìwèi zhe yǒu rén hái zài huódòng——kàn diànshì, zuò jiāwù, huòzhě zhǐ shì děng yī gè wǎn guī de jiārén. Lěng báisè de yíngguāng dēngguāng jīhū kěyǐ quèdìng shì zài jiābān——nà zhǒng dēngguāng yǒu yī zhǒng bú dài gǎnqíng de jūnyún míngliàng, xiàng shì bǎ hēiyè qiángxíng qiēgē chū yī kuài báitiān de suìpiàn. Zuì wēnróu de shì nà zhǒng ruòyǒuruòwú de lánsè wēiguāng, tōngcháng láizì shǒujī huò píngbǎn diànnǎo de píngmù, shuōmíng zhǔrén yǐjīng shàng chuáng dàn hái méiyǒu rùshuì, zhèngzài hēi'àn zhōng shuā zhe zuìhòu jǐ tiáo xiāoxi. Ǒu'ěr huì chūxiàn yī zhǒng kuàisù shǎnshuò de cǎisè guāng——nà shì yǒu rén zài shēnyè kàn diànyǐng huò dǎ yóuxì. Zuì ràng wǒ zháomí de shì nàxiē zài língchén liǎng sān diǎn tūrán liàng qǐ de dēng. Tāmen zǒng shì hěn tūwù, xiàng yèkōng zhōng huà guò de liúxīng, ràng rén rěn bú zhù cāicè bèihòu de yuányīn——shì bèi èmèng jīngxǐng? Shì yīng'ér de kū shēng? Háishi hūrán xiǎng qǐ le yī jiàn fàng bú xià de shì? Zhèxiē dēng tōngcháng zài shí jǐ fēnzhōng hòu xīmiè, fǎngfú nàge duǎnzàn de qīngxǐng zhǐ shì mèngjìng de yánshēn. Wǒ hòulái yìshí dào, wǒ zài zuò de shìqíng běnzhì shàng shì yī zhǒng chéngshì bǎn de guānxīng——měi yī shàn chuānghù dōu shì yī kē héngxīng, měi yī zhǎn dēng dōu shì tā cǐkè de guāngpǔ. Nǐ wúfǎ zhīdào nà kē xīngqiú shàng fāshēng le shénme jùtǐ de gùshi, dàn guāng de yánsè, liàngdù hé chíxù shíjiān yǐjīng zúyǐ gōulè chū yī gè shēnghuó de lúnkuò.
text_en: Looking across to the high-rise opposite late at night, every lit window is a miniature theater. I once spent an entire winter on my balcony observing the window-light changes in the building across the way, like an amateur astronomer without a telescope, discerning with the naked eye constellations that belong to the human world. Warm yellow light is the most common—usually a ceiling lamp or table lamp in the living room, meaning someone is still up: watching TV, doing housework, or just waiting for a family member who is late coming home. Cool white fluorescent light almost certainly means someone is working overtime—that kind of light has an emotionless, uniform brightness, as if forcibly cutting a shard of daytime out of the night. The gentlest is a faint, barely-there blue glow, usually from a phone or tablet screen, indicating the occupant has gone to bed but hasn't yet fallen asleep, scrolling through a last few messages in the dark. Occasionally a rapidly flickering colored light appears—someone watching a movie or playing games late at night. What fascinates me most are the lights that suddenly come on at two or three in the morning. They are always abrupt, like a shooting star streaking across the night sky, making one unable to resist guessing the reason behind them—woken by a nightmare? A baby's cry? Or suddenly remembering something one can't let go of? These lights usually go out after ten minutes or so, as if that brief wakefulness were merely an extension of a dream. I later realized that what I was doing was essentially a city version of stargazing—every window is a star, every lamp is its spectrum at this moment. You can't know what specific story is happening on that planet, but the color, brightness, and duration of the light are enough to sketch the outline of a life.

title: The Tenderness of Repair Shops
text_zh: 在快递驿站和外卖平台统治了街面的时代，城南那条小巷里还活着三家修理铺——修鞋的老周、修伞的林师傅、以及修钟表的何老太。他们像是城市新陈代谢系统里最后的抵抗者，坚守着一个正在消亡的信念：东西坏了应该修，而不是扔。老周的修鞋铺只有两平方米大小，工具挂满了墙壁，空气里弥漫着皮革和胶水的味道。他说现在来修鞋的人越来越少了，年轻人穿坏一双鞋的第一反应是打开购物App重新下单。「他们不是不珍惜东西，」老周说得很平静，「是这个时代教会了他们，新的永远比修过的便宜。」但老周的生意并没有因此消失，反而在最近几年出现了一种微妙的变化——来找他的人开始带着情感而不仅仅是鞋子。一个年轻女孩抱着一双已经磨得面目全非的帆布鞋来，说那是大学时男朋友送的，分手了但鞋还想留着。一个中年男人带来一双老式的手工布鞋，是他过世母亲做的最后一双。老周接过鞋的时候手特别轻，像是在接一件瓷器。他说他不知道从什么时候起，自己修的不再是鞋，而是别人的回忆。「鞋可以买新的，但缝在鞋底上的那些路是买不到的。我修的是那些路。」林师傅的修伞铺也有类似的故事，何老太修的钟表更是如此——每一块被送来的旧表背后，都有一段关于时间的私人叙事。这三家修理铺，在某种意义上，是这条小巷里最后的情感考古学家。
text_pinyin: Zài kuàidì yìzhàn hé wàimài píngtái tǒngzhì le jiēmiàn de shídài, chéng nán nà tiáo xiǎo xiàng lǐ hái huó zhe sān jiā xiūlǐ pù——xiū xié de Lǎo Zhōu, xiū sǎn de Lín shīfu, yǐjí xiū zhōngbiǎo de Hé lǎotài. Tāmen xiàng shì chéngshì xīnchén dàixiè xìtǒng lǐ zuìhòu de dǐkàng zhě, jiānshǒu zhe yī gè zhèngzài xiāowáng de xìnniàn: dōngxi huài le yīnggāi xiū, ér bú shì rēng. Lǎo Zhōu de xiū xié pù zhǐ yǒu liǎng píngfāng mǐ dàxiǎo, gōngjù guà mǎn le qiángbì, kōngqì lǐ mímàn zhe pígé hé jiāoshuǐ de wèidào. Tā shuō xiànzài lái xiū xié de rén yuèláiyuè shǎo le, niánqīngrén chuān huài yī shuāng xié de dì yī fǎnyìng shì dǎkāi gòuwù App chóngxīn xià dān. 「Tāmen bú shì bù zhēnxī dōngxi,」Lǎo Zhōu shuō de hěn píngjìng, 「shì zhège shídài jiàohuì le tāmen, xīn de yǒngyuǎn bǐ xiū guò de piányi.」Dàn Lǎo Zhōu de shēngyì bìng méiyǒu yīncǐ xiāoshī, fǎn'ér zài zuìjìn jǐ nián chūxiàn le yī zhǒng wēimiào de biànhuà——lái zhǎo tā de rén kāishǐ dài zhe qínggǎn ér bù jǐnjǐn shì xiézi. Yī gè niánqīng nǚhái bào zhe yī shuāng yǐjīng mó de miànmù quánfēi de fānbù xié lái, shuō nà shì dàxué shí nánpéngyǒu sòng de, fēnshǒu le dàn xié hái xiǎng liú zhe. Yī gè zhōngnián nánrén dài lái yī shuāng lǎoshì de shǒugōng bù xié, shì tā guòshì mǔqīn zuò de zuìhòu yī shuāng. Lǎo Zhōu jiē guò xié de shíhou shǒu tèbié qīng, xiàng shì zài jiē yī jiàn cíqì. Tā shuō tā bù zhīdào cóng shénme shíhou qǐ, zìjǐ xiū de bú zài shì xié, ér shì biérén de huíyì. 「Xié kěyǐ mǎi xīn de, dàn féng zài xiédǐ shàng de nàxiē lù shì mǎi bú dào de. Wǒ xiū de shì nàxiē lù.」Lín shīfu de xiū sǎn pù yě yǒu lèisì de gùshi, Hé lǎotài xiū de zhōngbiǎo gèng shì rúcǐ——měi yī kuài bèi sòng lái de jiù biǎo bèihòu, dōu yǒu yī duàn guānyú shíjiān de sīrén xùshì. Zhè sān jiā xiūlǐ pù, zài mǒu zhǒng yìyì shàng, shì zhè tiáo xiǎo xiàng lǐ zuìhòu de qínggǎn kǎogǔ xuéjiā.
text_en: In an era where parcel stations and delivery platforms rule the streetscape, three repair shops still survive in that alley in the south of the city—Old Zhou the cobbler, Master Lin the umbrella mender, and Granny He the watchmaker. They are like the last holdouts in the city's metabolic system, clinging to a belief that is dying: when something breaks, you should fix it, not throw it away. Old Zhou's cobbler shop is only two square meters, tools covering every wall, the air thick with the smell of leather and glue. He says fewer and fewer people come to have shoes repaired now; a young person's first reaction to wearing out a pair of shoes is to open a shopping app and reorder. 'It's not that they don't value things,' Old Zhou says calmly. 'It's that this era has taught them that new is always cheaper than repaired.' But Old Zhou's business hasn't disappeared; in recent years it has undergone a subtle transformation—the people who come to him now bring emotions, not just shoes. A young woman came cradling a pair of canvas shoes worn beyond recognition, saying her college boyfriend had given them to her; they'd broken up but she still wanted to keep the shoes. A middle-aged man brought a pair of old-fashioned handmade cloth shoes, the last pair his late mother ever made. When Old Zhou took the shoes his hands were unusually gentle, as if receiving a piece of porcelain. He says he doesn't know when it happened, but what he repairs is no longer shoes—it's other people's memories. 'You can buy new shoes, but you can't buy the roads stitched into the soles. I'm repairing those roads.' Master Lin's umbrella shop has similar stories, and Granny He's watches even more so—behind every old timepiece brought in lies a private narrative about time. These three repair shops are, in a sense, the last emotional archaeologists of this alley.

title: The Grammar of Alleyway Cats
text_zh: 这个老城区的流浪猫形成了一套精密的社会体系，而这套体系的运作逻辑竟然与人类社区的治理结构有着惊人的相似。巷口杂货店门前的那只橘猫是这片区域公认的「片区长」，它拥有整条巷子的优先觅食权和最好的午睡位置——杂货店老板娘放在门口的那把旧藤椅。其他猫想要穿过它的领地，必须遵循一套不成文的通行礼仪：低头、放慢脚步、不直视它的眼睛。如果有外来的猫闯入，橘猫并不会立刻发动攻击，而是会先在对方身边慢慢绕圈走，用体态语言完成一次「身份审查」。大多数外来者在这种无声的压力下会自行离开，极少数坚持留下的则需要经历几天的「观察期」——在此期间，它们只能在巷子边缘的几个指定位置活动。最让我惊叹的是猫群内部的互助机制。有一只瘸了一条腿的黑猫，其他猫吃完之后总会给它留一些食物，不是出于同情，更像是一种自然而然的默契。一位长年投喂流浪猫的邻居告诉我，猫的社会比人类想象的要复杂得多。「它们有自己的外交、自己的边境、自己的福利体系，」她说，「唯一跟人类不同的是，它们从不开会。」我有时候在想，也许我们在城市治理上遇到的很多难题，这些巷弄里的猫早就用自己的方式解决了——不需要条文，不需要投票，只需要在日复一日的相处中，磨合出一套所有成员都心照不宣的规则。
text_pinyin: Zhège lǎo chéngqū de liúlàng māo xíngchéng le yī tào jīngmì de shèhuì tǐxì, ér zhè tào tǐxì de yùnzuò luójí jìngrán yǔ rénlèi shèqū de zhìlǐ jiégòu yǒu zhe jīngrén de xiāngsì. Xiàng kǒu záhuòdiàn mén qián de nà zhī júmāo shì zhè piàn qūyù gōngrèn de「piànqūzhǎng」, tā yōngyǒu zhěng tiáo xiàngzi de yōuxiān mìshí quán hé zuìhǎo de wǔshuì wèizhì——záhuòdiàn lǎobǎnniáng fàng zài ménkǒu de nà bǎ jiù téngyǐ. Qítā māo xiǎng yào chuān guò tā de lǐngdì, bìxū zūnxún yī tào bù chéngwén de tōngxíng lǐyí: dītóu, fàng màn jiǎobù, bù zhíshì tā de yǎnjīng. Rúguǒ yǒu wàilái de māo chuǎngrù, júmāo bìng bú huì lìkè fādòng gōngjī, ér shì huì xiān zài duìfāng shēnbiān mànmàn rào quān zǒu, yòng tǐtài yǔyán wánchéng yī cì「shēnfèn shěnchá」. Dàduōshù wàilái zhě zài zhè zhǒng wúshēng de yālì xià huì zìxíng líkāi, jí shǎoshù jiānchí liú xià de zé xūyào jīnglì jǐ tiān de「guānchá qī」——zài cǐ qījiān, tāmen zhǐ néng zài xiàngzi biānyuán de jǐ gè zhǐdìng wèizhì huódòng. Zuì ràng wǒ jīngtàn de shì māo qún nèibù de hùzhù jīzhì. Yǒu yī zhī qué le yī tiáo tuǐ de hēi māo, qítā māo chī wán zhīhòu zǒng huì gěi tā liú yīxiē shíwù, bú shì chūyú tóngqíng, gèng xiàng shì yī zhǒng zìrán ér rán de mòqì. Yī wèi cháng nián tóuwèi liúlàng māo de línjū gàosu wǒ, māo de shèhuì bǐ rénlèi xiǎngxiàng de yào fùzá de duō. 「Tāmen yǒu zìjǐ de wàijiāo, zìjǐ de biānjìng, zìjǐ de fúlì tǐxì,」tā shuō, 「wéiyī gēn rénlèi bùtóng de shì, tāmen cóng bù kāihuì.」Wǒ yǒu shíhou zài xiǎng, yěxǔ wǒmen zài chéngshì zhìlǐ shàng yù dào de hěn duō nántí, zhèxiē xiàngnòng lǐ de māo zǎo jiù yòng zìjǐ de fāngshì jiějué le——bù xūyào tiáowén, bù xūyào tóupiào, zhǐ xūyào zài rì fù yī rì de xiāngchǔ zhōng, móhé chū yī tào suǒyǒu chéngyuán dōu xīnzhào bú xuān de guīzé.
text_en: The stray cats of this old district have formed an intricate social system, and the operating logic of this system bears a startling resemblance to the governance structure of human communities. The orange tabby in front of the corner shop is the recognized 'district chief' of this area, enjoying priority foraging rights over the entire alley and the best napping spot—the old rattan chair the shopkeeper's wife leaves by the door. Other cats wishing to pass through its territory must observe an unwritten code of transit etiquette: lower the head, slow the pace, avoid direct eye contact. When an outside cat intrudes, the orange tabby doesn't immediately attack but first walks slowly in circles around the newcomer, completing an 'identity inspection' through body language. Most outsiders leave of their own accord under this silent pressure; the rare few who insist on staying must undergo several days of an 'observation period'—during which they may only move about in a few designated spots at the alley's margins. What astonishes me most is the mutual-aid mechanism within the cat community. There is a black cat with a lame leg, and the other cats always leave some food for it after eating—not out of pity, but more like a natural, unspoken understanding. A neighbor who has fed the strays for years told me that cat society is far more complex than humans imagine. 'They have their own diplomacy, their own borders, their own welfare system,' she said. 'The only difference from humans is they never hold meetings.' Sometimes I think that many of the difficulties we encounter in urban governance, these alleyway cats long ago solved in their own way—no regulations needed, no voting required, just the daily friction of living together, gradually polishing a set of rules that all members tacitly understand.

title: The Retirement of a Phone Booth
text_zh: 市政府去年宣布将分批拆除全市剩余的两百三十个公共电话亭，理由是维护成本远超使用率。消息传出后，一个叫「电话亭守护者联盟」的民间团体在社交媒体上发起了一场保留运动，收集了四千多条关于电话亭的个人记忆。其中有一条来自一位中学语文老师的投稿让我久久不能忘怀。她说她上大学的时候，每个周末都会去学校门口的电话亭给远在农村的父母打电话。那时候手机还没有普及，打长途要用磁卡，一张五十元的磁卡大约能通话十五分钟。为了在有限的时间里说完所有想说的话，她每次打电话之前都会在纸上列一个提纲——先汇报学习成绩，再问家里的庄稼收成，最后用剩下的两三分钟聊一些无关紧要的家常。她说正是那种时间的紧迫感，让每一句话都变得慎重而珍贵。「现在微信聊天可以说一天，反而什么都没说，」她写道，「那时候十五分钟要把全部的想念压缩进去，每一个字都是经过筛选的。那种密度的交流，在即时通讯的时代已经不存在了。」另一位投稿者是个出租车司机，他说二十年前他第一次从车站出来到一座陌生的城市闯荡，身上只有一百块钱。他在火车站广场的电话亭里给老家的朋友打了一个电话，那通电话帮他找到了第一份工作。「那个电话亭是我在这座城市扎根的起点，」他说，「拆了它，就像拆了我人生的第一块地基。」
text_pinyin: Shì zhèngfǔ qùnián xuānbù jiāng fēn pī chāichú quán shì shèngyú de liǎng bǎi sānshí gè gōnggòng diànhuàtíng, lǐyóu shì wéihù chéngběn yuǎn chāo shǐyònglǜ. Xiāoxi chuánchū hòu, yī gè jiào「Diànhuàtíng Shǒuhù Zhě Liánméng」de mínjiān tuántǐ zài shèjiāo méitǐ shàng fāqǐ le yī chǎng bǎoliú yùndòng, shōují le sìqiān duō tiáo guānyú diànhuàtíng de gèrén jìyì. Qízhōng yǒu yī tiáo láizì yī wèi zhōngxué yǔwén lǎoshī de tóugǎo ràng wǒ jiǔjiǔ bù néng wànghuái. Tā shuō tā shàng dàxué de shíhou, měi gè zhōumò dōu huì qù xuéxiào ménkǒu de diànhuàtíng gěi yuǎn zài nóngcūn de fùmǔ dǎ diànhuà. Nà shíhou shǒujī hái méiyǒu pǔjí, dǎ chángtú yào yòng cíkǎ, yī zhāng wǔshí yuán de cíkǎ dàyuē néng tōnghuà shíwǔ fēnzhōng. Wèile zài yǒuxiàn de shíjiān lǐ shuō wán suǒyǒu xiǎng shuō de huà, tā měi cì dǎ diànhuà zhīqián dōu huì zài zhǐ shàng liè yī gè tígāng——xiān huìbào xuéxí chéngjì, zài wèn jiā lǐ de zhuāngjia shōuchéng, zuìhòu yòng shèng xià de liǎng sān fēnzhōng liáo yīxiē wúguān jǐnyào de jiācháng. Tā shuō zhèng shì nà zhǒng shíjiān de jǐnpògǎn, ràng měi yī jù huà dōu biàn de shènzhòng ér zhēnguì. 「Xiànzài Wēixìn liáotiān kěyǐ shuō yī tiān, fǎn'ér shénme dōu méi shuō,」tā xiě dào, 「nà shíhou shíwǔ fēnzhōng yào bǎ quánbù de xiǎngniàn yāsuō jìnqù, měi yī gè zì dōu shì jīngguò shāixuǎn de. Nà zhǒng mìdù de jiāoliú, zài jíshí tōngxùn de shídài yǐjīng bù cúnzài le.」Lìng yī wèi tóugǎo zhě shì gè chūzūchē sījī, tā shuō èrshí nián qián tā dì yī cì cóng chēzhàn chūlái dào yī zuò mòshēng de chéngshì chuǎngdàng, shēn shàng zhǐ yǒu yībǎi kuài qián. Tā zài huǒchēzhàn guǎngchǎng de diànhuàtíng lǐ gěi lǎojiā de péngyǒu dǎ le yī gè diànhuà, nà tōng diànhuà bāng tā zhǎo dào le dì yī fèn gōngzuò. 「Nàge diànhuàtíng shì wǒ zài zhè zuò chéngshì zhā gēn de qǐdiǎn,」tā shuō, 「chāi le tā, jiù xiàng chāi le wǒ rénshēng de dì yī kuài dìjī.」
text_en: The municipal government announced last year it would dismantle the city's remaining two hundred and thirty public phone booths in batches, citing maintenance costs far exceeding usage rates. After the news broke, a grassroots group called the 'Phone Booth Guardians Alliance' launched a preservation campaign on social media, collecting over four thousand personal memories about phone booths. One submission, from a middle-school Chinese teacher, has stayed with me a long time. She said that when she was in college, every weekend she would go to the phone booth outside the school gate to call her parents far away in the countryside. Mobile phones were not yet widespread then; long-distance calls required magnetic cards, and a fifty-yuan card allowed about fifteen minutes of talk time. To say everything she wanted within the limited time, she would outline a script on paper before each call—first report her grades, then ask about the harvest at home, and finally use the remaining two or three minutes for inconsequential small talk. She said it was precisely that urgency of time that made every sentence deliberate and precious. 'Now you can chat on WeChat for an entire day and end up saying nothing,' she wrote. 'Back then you had to compress all your longing into fifteen minutes; every word was carefully chosen. That density of communication no longer exists in the age of instant messaging.' Another contributor was a taxi driver. He said that twenty years ago, the first time he stepped out of the train station into a strange city to make his fortune, he had only a hundred yuan on him. He made a call from the phone booth in the station square to a friend back home, and that call helped him find his first job. 'That phone booth was the starting point of my putting down roots in this city,' he said. 'Tearing it down is like tearing out the first foundation stone of my life.'

title: The Diplomacy of Shared Walls
text_zh: 在老式的筒子楼里，邻居之间只隔着一面薄薄的砖墙，隔音效果几乎为零。这意味着你的生活没有任何声学上的隐私——你的闹钟几点响、你炒菜用什么火候、你多久给父母打一次电话、你晚上几点睡觉，隔壁邻居全都一清二楚。在这种极端的居住密度下，住户之间发展出了一套精妙的「共墙外交」协议。最基本的条款是时间公约——晚上十点以后不用洗衣机，早上七点以前不用榨汁机，周末午休时段不打电动工具。这些规则从未被正式讨论或签署，而是在数十年的磨合中自然演化出来的。更微妙的是声音的「已读不回」原则——即使你清清楚楚听到了隔壁夫妻吵架的全部内容，第二天在楼道里碰面的时候也要表现得完全不知情。这种装聋作哑的默契，是比任何隔音材料都有效的心理墙壁。最让我感动的是那些在墙壁另一侧传来的善意。冬天的夜晚，当你咳嗽不止的时候，会听到隔壁轻轻敲了三下墙，这是「你还好吗」的意思。如果你回敲两下，意思是「我没事」。如果你不回应，十分钟之内对方一定会来敲你的门。这套敲墙暗号是我搬进来的第一天，隔壁的刘大姐教给我的。她说：「住在这种房子里，墙不是用来隔开人的，是用来连接人的。你只要学会在需要的时候敲三下，这面墙就是全世界最好的邻居。」
text_pinyin: Zài lǎoshì de tǒngzilóu lǐ, línjū zhījiān zhǐ gé zhe yī miàn báo báo de zhuānqiáng, géyīn xiàoguǒ jīhū wéi líng. Zhè yìwèi zhe nǐ de shēnghuó méiyǒu rènhé shēngxué shàng de yǐnsī——nǐ de nàozhōng jǐ diǎn xiǎng, nǐ chǎo cài yòng shénme huǒhòu, nǐ duō jiǔ gěi fùmǔ dǎ yī cì diànhuà, nǐ wǎnshàng jǐ diǎn shuìjiào, gébì línjū quán dōu yīqīng'èrchǔ. Zài zhè zhǒng jíduān de jūzhù mìdù xià, zhùhù zhījiān fāzhǎn chū le yī tào jīngmiào de「gòng qiáng wàijiāo」xiéyì. Zuì jīběn de tiáokuǎn shì shíjiān gōngyuē——wǎnshàng shí diǎn yǐhòu bú yòng xǐyījī, zǎoshang qī diǎn yǐqián bú yòng zhàzhījī, zhōumò wǔxiū shíduàn bù dǎ diàndòng gōngjù. Zhèxiē guīzé cóng wèi bèi zhèngshì tǎolùn huò qiānshǔ, ér shì zài shù shí nián de móhé zhōng zìrán yǎnhuà chūlái de. Gèng wēimiào de shì shēngyīn de「yǐ dú bù huí」yuánzé——jíshǐ nǐ qīngqīng chǔchǔ tīng dào le gébì fūqī chǎojià de quánbù nèiróng, dì èr tiān zài lóudào lǐ pèngmiàn de shíhou yě yào biǎoxiàn de wánquán bù zhīqíng. Zhè zhǒng zhuāng lóng zuò yǎ de mòqì, shì bǐ rènhé géyīn cáiliào dōu yǒuxiào de xīnlǐ qiángbì. Zuì ràng wǒ gǎndòng de shì nàxiē zài qiángbì lìng yī cè chuán lái de shànyì. Dōngtiān de yèwǎn, dāng nǐ késou bù zhǐ de shíhou, huì tīng dào gébì qīngqīng qiāo le sān xià qiáng, zhè shì「nǐ hái hǎo ma」de yìsi. Rúguǒ nǐ huí qiāo liǎng xià, yìsi shì「wǒ méi shì」. Rúguǒ nǐ bù huíyìng, shí fēnzhōng zhī nèi duìfāng yīdìng huì lái qiāo nǐ de mén. Zhè tào qiāo qiáng ànhào shì wǒ bān jìnlái de dì yī tiān, gébì de Liú dàjiě jiào gěi wǒ de. Tā shuō: 「Zhù zài zhè zhǒng fángzi lǐ, qiáng bú shì yòng lái gé kāi rén de, shì yòng lái liánjiē rén de. Nǐ zhǐyào xuéhuì zài xūyào de shíhou qiāo sān xià, zhè miàn qiáng jiù shì quán shìjiè zuìhǎo de línjū.」
text_en: In the old-style tube apartments, neighbors are separated by only a thin brick wall with virtually zero soundproofing. This means your life has no acoustic privacy whatsoever—what time your alarm goes off, how high you set the flame when stir-frying, how often you call your parents, what time you go to bed at night—your next-door neighbor knows it all. Under this extreme residential density, occupants have developed an exquisite 'shared-wall diplomacy' protocol. The most basic provision is a time convention—no washing machine after ten at night, no juicer before seven in the morning, no power tools during weekend afternoon naps. These rules were never formally discussed or signed; they evolved naturally over decades of accommodation. More subtle is the 'read but don't reply' principle regarding sound—even if you heard every word of the couple next door's argument with crystal clarity, when you meet in the hallway the next day you must act completely ignorant. This feigned deafness is a psychological wall more effective than any soundproofing material. What moves me most is the goodwill transmitted through the other side of the wall. On winter nights, when you can't stop coughing, you'll hear three gentle knocks on the wall from next door—meaning 'are you all right?' If you knock back twice, it means 'I'm fine.' If you don't respond, within ten minutes the other person will certainly come knocking on your door. This wall-knocking code was taught to me by Big Sister Liu next door on the very first day I moved in. She said: 'Living in this kind of building, the wall isn't there to separate people—it's there to connect them. As long as you learn to knock three times when you need to, this wall is the best neighbor in the world.'
